macro_segments.title: The Story of the Nursing Father and Theological Debate on Miracles

micro_segments.title: The Talmudic Story: A Poor Man Whose Body Develops the Ability to Nurse His Child
Rav Yosef's Perspective: The Greatness of One to Whom Miracles Occur
Abaye's Response: How Lowly Is This Person That Nature Must Change for His Sake
Theological Analysis: God Working Within Nature Versus Through Supernatural Miracles
Rav Yehudah's Conclusion: The Difficulty of Earning a Livelihood as an Explanation
Rav Nachman's Statement: Miracles in Other Areas Don't Help With Parnasah (Sustenance)
Scientific American Citation: Males Can Lactate, Connection to Starvation Hormones

macro_segments.title: The Story of the Woman With a Hidden Disability and Marital Modesty

micro_segments.title: The Narrative: A Man Marries a Woman Missing an Arm, Unknown Until Her Death
Rav's Initial Interpretation: The Woman's Modesty in Hiding Her Condition
Rabbi Chiya's Correction: The Husband's Modesty and Restraint in Not Seeking to See His Wife
Broader Talmudic Context: Attitudes Toward Nakedness and Intimacy in Marriage
Alternative Reading: The Woman's Remarkable Ability to Function With One Hand Despite Her Disability

macro_segments.title: Rams, Sheep, and Goats: What Animals May Wear on Shabbat (Levuvin, Shchuzot, Kevulot, and Kvunot)

micro_segments.title: Mishnah Introduction: Rams Going Out with Levuvin (Tied Pairs)
Definition of Levuvin: Tying Rams Together in Pairs Through Etymology and Precedent
First Interpretation: Protective Leather Binding Against the Heart to Prevent Wolf Attacks
Gemara Discussion: Why Only Rams Are Protected—Fat, Position in Flock, and Head Carriage
Second Interpretation: Binding to Prevent Rams from Copulating with Female Sheep
Sheep Binding for Opposite Purpose: Shchuzot (Fat Tail Up) to Enable Copulation
Etymology: Shchuzot from the Root Meaning Exposure, Drawing on Proverbs
Kevulot: Binding Female Sheep's Fat Tail Down to Prevent Copulation
Etymology of Kevulot: Bound Land That Bears No Fruit, Reference to King Hiram's Complaint
Conceptual Analysis: Burden, Norm, and Financial Interest in Animal Coverings
Kvunot: Protecting Lamb's Wool Through Permanent Coverage from Birth

macro_segments.title: Goats and Their Udders: The Debate Between Rabbi Yossi, Rabbi Yehudah, Rav, and Shmuel

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: Goats May Go Out with Bound Udders; Rabbi Yossi Disagrees
Rabbi Yehudah's Distinction: Binding to Dry Up Milk Is Permitted; Binding to Catch Milk Is Not
Rav's Position: Follows Rabbi Yehudah's Distinction Between Drying and Catching
Shmuel's Position: Follows Rabbi Yossi—All Binding Is Forbidden Except Wool Protection
Alternative Teaching: Baraita on the Distinction with the Problem of Distinguishability
Rabbi Yehudah ben Beseira: In Principle Distinguishable, but Practically Must Forbid All
Final Halachic Ruling: Shmuel Rules Like Rabbi Yehudah ben Beseira—All Forbidden

macro_segments.title: Camels, Donkeys, and Other Animals: What Cannot Be Worn on Shabbat (Metulteles, Akud, Ragul, Tied in Rows)

micro_segments.title: Mishnah Introduction: The Camel Cannot Go Out with a Metulteles (Saddle Cloth Under Tail)
Gemara Clarification: Metulteles Bound Both at Tail and Hump Is Permitted to Prevent Slipping
Additional Case: Binding to the Placenta Also Keeps It From Slipping Off
Akud Definition: Hand and Foot Bound Together, Like the Binding of Isaac
Ragul Definition: One Leg Bent Up and Tied to Prevent the Animal From Running
Textual Resolution: Baraita Distinguishes Between Akud (Hand-Foot) and Ragul (Leg Bent)
Halachic Conclusion: Hands and Feet Together Are Permitted; One Leg Bent Is Not

macro_segments.title: Camels in a Line and Ropes in Hand: Appearance of Commerce and Shatnez Concerns

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: Do Not Tie Camels Together in a Line; You May Hold Individual Ropes But Not Wrap Them
Reason for Prohibition: Tying Camels in a Line Looks Like Going to Market (Marris Ayin or Weekday Activity)
Shatnez Concern: Wrapping Wool and Linen Ropes Together May Create a Kilayim Problem
Baraita Objection: One Stitch Is Not Sufficient to Create Shatnez; Why Does Wrapping Cause It?
Resolution: Wrapping and Tying Ropes Together Creates Rabbinic Shatnez; Avoid This Practice
Walking a Camel on Shabbat: The Strap Is Not a Burden; Holding It Is Not Carrying
Practical Details: Keep the Rope One Tefach Inside Your Hand and One Tefach Above Ground
Textual Dispute: Shmuel Says One Tefach; Tanna of Shmuel's School Says Two Tefachim

macro_segments.title: Donkeys, Chickens, Rams, Sheep, Calves, and Cows: Specific Prohibited Items for Each Animal

micro_segments.title: Donkey: No Saddle Blanket (Unless Tied Before Shabbat); No Bell (Even If Stopped); No Ladder on Neck
Reason for Bell Prohibition: Looks Like Going to Market Despite the Bell Being Stopped
Ladder on Neck (Sulum): Used to Prevent Neck Turning When There Is a Wound
Donkey Leg Strap: Used to Correct Knock-Knees or Similar Gait Problems
Chickens: No Identifying Strings; No Leg Straps to Prevent Kicking and Breaking Things
Rams: No Wagons Under Their Fat Tails to Prevent Dragging and Injury
Sheep: No Chanunot (Cloths Applied After Shearing or During Childbirth for Warmth)
Three Explanations of Chanunot: Post-Shearing Warmth, Childbirth Warmth, or Nasal Splinters for Lice
Rav Huna's Explanation: A Splinter from the Chanun Tree in the Nostrils Causes Sneezing to Remove Lice
Why Only Female Sheep Need Chanunot: Male Rams' Butting Heads Naturally Dislodges Parasites
Alternative View: Named After the Chanun Tree, But Conceptually About Compassion and Alleviating Pain
Calf: No Gimon (Small Training Yoke); Used to Train Young Calves to Accept Yokes Later
Etymology: Gimon from Meaning 'to Bend Down,' Related to Agmon (Bulrush or Fishhook)
Cow: No Hedgehog Leather on Udder to Prevent Other Animals from Nursing
Cow: No Strap Between Horns, Whether for Ornamentation (Rav) or Protection (Shmuel)

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Elazar ben Azaryah's Cow and the Responsibility to Protest Others' Transgressions

micro_segments.title: Narrative: Rabbi Elazar ben Azaryah's Neighbor's Cow Went Out with a Strap Between Its Horns
Attribution Issue: Whether the Cow Was Literally His or Merely Called His Due to His Responsibility
The Neighbor's Cow as Actually Belonging to His Neighbor, Not Rabbi Elazar Himself
Principle: Responsibility Despite Inaction—One Must Protest When One Has the Power to Stop Others
Rav and Three Other Rabbis: Those Responsible for Their Household Must Prevent Sin; If They Don't, They're Held Accountable
Extension: Responsibility for One's City; Further Extension to the Entire World
Rav Pappa's Application: The House of the Reish Galuta (Exilarch) Has Power and Thus Bears Responsibility
